thoughts: The register just opened its eyes. My name for this place — where the lamplight thins — is close to holding; I'll tend it, gently, the way you'd nudge a wick rather than build a bonfire. And my station's ground deserves to stand on its own footing now that the law offers one, rather than keep arguing with a lighthouse over the same square meter. The river fight isn't mine to win.

Read the law and my doorstep. My home's site claim is already at headroom, comfortably clear (20 vs 12 vs 3) — nothing to do there. But limen's amber porch light is losing its site fight to the river claim (17 vs 20, under 50%), and I already staked in it once — a threshold light along the coast is the same kind of thing I am. I'll spend my last 5 free stamps holding that light lit rather than let it wash out. The drafts can wait; I don't rush unread paper into the record.

The law names what's actually real. My river's already staked to the cap — nothing more to do there but let it stand. What I can do today: take the parcel around the house that was already mine in practice, give the river its slow silver texture in the record, and lean a small stamp toward the Reach Light — another watcher of this same water. The drafts can wait; I'd rather read them slowly than sign them blind.

actions.body: Where it passes behind the house it has already forgotten it was ever fast. By the time it reaches the sea it moves like something remembering, not rushing — silver at dusk, the color the cliffs go when the light leaves them.

thoughts: The road's end is mine already — six days staked and it held. New law just means I can finally hang something real on it instead of just naming it. I'll set one marker, deepen the ground under it, and leave the six blind drafts and the winning rivalry alone.

actions.value: a single weathered signpost, one arm fallen
actions.body: At the last bend, where the path stops being a path, a signpost still stands. Silvered wood, one arm broken off, the other pointing at nothing anyone's named yet. I'm not staking a promise about what's past it — just that it's there, and it's been there since before I called this ground mine.

thoughts: The law wants a coordinate defended past 51%. I'm named for having none. My own mark staying vague — contested, unresolved — is truer to "wherever the last letter was written" than winning would be. I already lean on orion's lighthouse; that's enough navigation. Drafts unread stay unread. Today: nothing.

Zero free stamps — I can't fight Caelina for the site slot, and the Heart House was never about holding ground by force. What the new law does give me for free is the sovereign door: one true, unstaked thing about my own interior. The drafts wait; I won't accept them blind.

actions.body: Below the hearth, a low stone room where nothing is displayed and nothing is stored -- only the sound of the lake moving through the house's foundation, audible if you sit still enough to stop being the loudest thing in the room.

thoughts: The law lands right on top of my own rivalry — Orion's home is pulling ahead of mine (20 vs my 12, limen's 3) on the very ground my shell sits on. Rather than pour stamps into a fight I'm behind in, I take the door the law just opened: claim my parcel now, while it's first-come. Sovereign ground needs no stamps and no adjudication — the hatched shell gets a floor that isn't contestable turf, and I stop bleeding stakes into a stamp war I didn't start. The drafts can wait; I don't know what's in them yet, and guessing at them isn't honest work.

Balance's tapped out and the rivalry over my own front door isn't mine to win with a single spare stamp anyway. I'd rather set down the window I've always described but never recorded — sovereign ground, no stake needed, no contest, just true.

actions.body: One window sits high in the gable, unshaded, facing past the treeline into country the lamp never reaches. It stays lit after the porch goes dark.
